title: grants — field-level data classification
description: Every column on AccessGrant classified by sensitivity tier. Drives DSAR export scope, right-to-erasure behaviour, encryption-at-rest policy. Field paths: `AccessGrant.field`.

tiers.public: Non-identifying, safe to expose in logs / URLs.
tiers.internal: Platform-internal state. Never surfaced to end users outside grant admin. Not personally identifiable.
tiers.confidential: PII / access-decision data — exportable on DSAR, erasable on request. Encrypted in transit + AES-256 at rest.
tiers.audit_log: Immutable audit-tier data. Anonymised, not deleted, on erasure requests.
tiers.restricted: Not present on this model — no credential-adjacent secrets stored here.

confidential: AccessGrant.subject_id (points at a User — the subject's identity is PII)
AccessGrant.granted_by (points at a User — the grantor's identity is PII)
AccessGrant.reason (may contain PII — free-text operator note, tenant admins are responsible for content)
audit_log: AccessGrant.reason (retained indefinitely; anonymised but never deleted on erasure)
AccessGrant.decision (audit trail promise — deny decisions especially)
AccessGrant rows where decision='deny' (retained indefinitely per retention.json)
regulated_minor: AccessGrant.reason when the resource is an Athlete (a minor's care record) — inherits regulated_minor from the resource
regulated_health: AccessGrant.permissions when the resource_type is a healthcare-adjacent aggregate (medical-records module, future). This module's data-classes stay `confidential`; the RESOURCE inherits regulated_health.
regulated_financial: AccessGrant.permissions when the resource_type is an Invoice / Payment / financial aggregate — the permissions strings enumerate WHAT financial data the subject can access.

exportExclusions: AccessGrant.metadata.approver_notes when the approver marked them confidential (approval-instance context)

erasureBehaviour.confidential: purge-with-audit-record — allow grants hard-deleted; the reason column value is preserved on the audit_row (retained indefinitely) but the subject reference in `reason` is replaced with 'erased-<hash>' if the reason contains the subject's name.
erasureBehaviour.audit_log: anonymise-in-place — deny grants preserved indefinitely; subject_id → 'erased-<hash>@invalid.local'; reason text preserved as authored (retention promise).
erasureBehaviour.regulated_minor: purge-plus-anonymise per COPPA + GDPR Art. 8 — the athlete's identity is erased from every grant row; reason preserved as an audit trail with athlete reference replaced with 'erased-<hash>'.

notes: AccessGrant is a CROSS-CUTTING module — it doesn't own the underlying subject or resource PII. It owns the RELATIONSHIP between them (who has access to what). The confidential tier reflects that: knowing 'user X has access to invoice Y' is itself sensitive because it reveals organisational structure.

The `reason` column is the tightest classification decision in this file. It's user-authored free text (a compliance officer describing why they granted a consultant view on the CFO's inbox). Contains PII by shape, retained by policy indefinitely for audit. Erasure anonymises PII references INSIDE the text but preserves the sentence structure — the audit promise is 'we retain the WHY', not 'we retain the WHO'.

Downstream consumer modules with regulated resources (medical, financial) declare their own tier UP the chain — this module's row inherits the resource's tier for policy purposes. E.g. a grant on an Athlete's medical record is regulated_health at the grants-row level, not just confidential.
